title: Wedging clay for hand-building
subtitle: Preparing clay so it works without trapping air or tearing
excerpt: Wedging homogenises the clay body and drives out air pockets before any hand-building project starts. A five-minute wedge on a plaster bat or canvas board transforms stiff or uneven clay into a responsive, air-free mass ready for pinching, coiling, or slab-rolling.
sourceNotes: Wedging is a fundamental clay preparation technique documented across pottery instruction since at least the early twentieth century. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Kneading clay to homogenise its water content and drive out trapped air bubbles before working.
A trapped bubble of air inside a clay body. In kiln-fired work, air pockets cause pieces to explode; in air-dry clay they cause surface cracks as the clay shrinks.
A flat slab of plaster of Paris used as a wedging and drying surface. The plaster absorbs moisture from the clay, making it easier to work and preventing sticking.

content.text: Before any hand-building project, the clay needs
wedging

: a repeated pressing and folding that removes uneven moisture, cold spots, and trapped air. Air-dry clay from a freshly opened packet is usually uniform enough; clay from a half-used bag often has a drier outer skin and a wetter core. Wedging takes five minutes and makes every step that follows easier.
When to use it

Wedge air-dry clay and paper clay before every session. If the clay was stored partly exposed and has a dry crust, slice the crust away with a wire cutter before wedging. For polymer clay, warm-and-condition replaces wedging (see the polymer clay conditioning technique).
What you need
A block of air-dry clay or paper clay, about 200-500 g
A canvas-covered board, smooth wooden board, or
plaster bat
as the work surface
Wire cutter for slicing the block
Step-by-step
1. Slice and check

Cut the clay block in half with the wire cutter. Look at the exposed cross-section: uniform colour and texture means the clay is even; pale or dark patches mean uneven moisture. Air pockets show as dimples or holes. Either issue is resolved by wedging.
2. Rams-head press

Reassemble the block. Place both palms on top, fingers pointing away from you, and press down and forward in one firm stroke, rocking the clay onto the board as if rolling it away. Lift the back edge of the clay up toward you, rotating the clay 90 degrees, and press again. The rhythm is: press forward, lift back edge, rotate a quarter-turn, repeat. Use your body weight, not just arm strength. After 20-30 strokes, the clay should feel uniformly soft throughout.
3. Confirm with a slice
Cut the block in half again with the wire cutter and check the cross-section. A well-wedged block shows uniform colour across the cut face, no distinct patches, and no visible
air pockets
. Rejoin the halves and proceed to the forming step.
What it looks like when it is right

The wedged block feels consistent from centre to edge when pressed with a thumb. The surface has a smooth, slightly matte sheen without dry patches or sticky wet spots. A wire cut through the middle shows no voids.
Common mistakes
Folding instead of pressing. Folding traps air rather than expelling it. The press-and-roll motion keeps the clay moving as a continuous mass.
Working on a slippery surface. The clay slides rather than grips. Canvas or a plaster bat gives enough traction for the clay to compress against the board.
Stopping too soon. Twenty strokes on fresh clay from a newly opened packet is usually enough. Clay that has been stored open may need 40-50 strokes to re-homogenise.
Adding water to dry clay during wedging. A few drops help, but water sprinkled on the outside of a dry block rarely penetrates. Wet the clay, seal it in a bag, and leave it overnight. Then wedge.
Diagram note

A cross-section diagram of the rams-head press shows the clay tilted at about 30 degrees on the board during the forward press. The back edge lifts; the front edge stays in contact with the board. The cross-section after a good wedge shows uniform texture throughout, compared to the mottled patches visible in the unwedged cross-section.

items.symptom: Clay tears on the board during wedging
items.cause: Clay is too dry to deform smoothly under pressure
items.fix: Seal in a plastic bag with a damp cloth and leave for several hours before trying again

items.symptom: Clay sticks to the board and tears
items.cause: Surface is too smooth and non-porous
items.fix: Switch to canvas or a plaster bat, which provide both grip and slight moisture absorption

items.cause: Clay was very unevenly stored or has a large dry crust
items.fix: Cut the block into four pieces, press them together, and wedge the combined block again
